q: This code panics at runtime. What is the bug?

opts.t: counts is a nil map; writing to it panics
map[string]int should be map[string]float64
opts.why: The type is fine; the problem is the map was never initialized.

opts.t: The key must be an int, not a string
opts.why: String keys are valid; the panic is caused by writing to an uninitialized nil map.

why: var m map[K]V declares a nil map. Reading from it is safe (returns zero value), but any write triggers a runtime panic. Fix: counts := make(map[string]int).
fj: "The type must be wrong."
fs: "var only declares the header — make allocates the hash table; nil map writes always panic."

q: This goroutine leaks because it blocks forever. Pick the fix.

opts.t: Pass a context.Context and select on ctx.Done() to stop the goroutine
Close ch inside the goroutine after the loop
opts.why: The loop is infinite, so close is unreachable; the goroutine still leaks.

opts.t: Make the channel buffered: make(chan int, 100)
opts.why: A buffer delays but does not prevent the leak; the goroutine still blocks when the buffer fills and the caller stops reading.

opts.t: Add time.Sleep(time.Second) inside the loop
opts.why: Sleep slows production but does not provide a way to stop the goroutine.

why: Without a cancellation signal the goroutine blocks on ch <- i forever once the caller stops reading. Accepting a context.Context and selecting on ctx.Done() lets the caller cancel cleanly.
fj: "Just buffer the channel bigger."
fs: "Any long-running goroutine needs a stop signal — context is the idiomatic way."

q: All goroutines print the same number. What is the bug? (Go < 1.22)

opts.t: The closure captures i by reference; by the time goroutines run, i is 3
Goroutines run before the loop increments i
opts.why: Goroutines are scheduled concurrently; the loop usually finishes first, so all see the final value.

opts.t: fmt.Println is not goroutine-safe
opts.why: fmt.Println is safe for concurrent use; the data race is on i, not on Println.

why: In Go < 1.22, all loop iterations share one i variable. The closures capture the variable itself, not a snapshot. When the goroutines execute, i has advanced to 3. Fix: pass i as an argument — go func(n int) { fmt.Println(n) }(i).
fj: "Goroutines print as they start, so they should see 0, 1, 2."
fs: "The closure captures the variable, not the value — shadow it or pass it as an arg."

q: Modifying b unexpectedly changes a. What is wrong?

opts.t: b shares a's backing array; append writes into a's unused capacity
append always allocates a new array, so this is a separate bug
opts.why: append only allocates when len == cap; here cap=4 > len=2, so no allocation occurs.

opts.t: The slice header for a is copied, making them independent
opts.why: Slice headers are independent, but both still point to the same underlying array.

why: b := a[:2] gives b len=2 but cap=4 pointing to the same array. append finds free capacity and writes 99 at index 2, which is a[2]. Use a[:2:2] to set cap=len, forcing a copy on append.
fj: "append always makes a copy."
fs: "Three-index slice a[:2:2] caps b's capacity, guaranteeing append allocates a fresh array."

q: The error check misses wrapped errors in production. Pick the fix.

opts.t: Replace == with errors.Is(err, ErrNotFound)
Use err.Error() == "not found" instead
opts.why: String comparison breaks if the message ever changes and still ignores wrapping.

opts.t: Change ErrNotFound to a struct type so == works
opts.why: Struct comparison still fails when the error is wrapped with fmt.Errorf("%w", err).

why: When fetchRecord wraps the error — e.g., fmt.Errorf("db: %w", ErrNotFound) — direct == comparison returns false because the pointers differ. errors.Is traverses the Unwrap chain and correctly matches the sentinel.
fj: "== is fine for sentinel errors."
fs: "Wrapping is idiomatic in Go; always use errors.Is so the check survives wrapping."

q: This loop leaks file descriptors. What is the bug?

opts.t: defer is scoped to the function, not the loop; all files stay open until readAll returns
os.Open should be os.Create to get write access
opts.why: Open is correct for reading; the bug is defer accumulating across iterations.

opts.t: process(f) should run inside a goroutine
opts.why: Concurrency is irrelevant here; the leak happens regardless of whether process is concurrent.

why: defer runs when the surrounding function returns, not at the end of each loop iteration. With many paths, all file descriptors accumulate until readAll exits. Fix: wrap the body in an IIFE — func() { f, _ := os.Open(p); defer f.Close(); process(f) }() — or call f.Close() explicitly.
fj: "defer closes the file after each iteration."
fs: "defer is function-scoped; wrap the body in a closure or call Close() directly after process."

q: What does this program print?

opts.t: timeout
done
opts.why: The goroutine sleeps 5 s but time.After fires after 1 s, so the timeout case wins.

opts.t: Deadlock — select blocks both cases
opts.why: select picks whichever case is ready first; time.After(1s) fires well before the 5 s goroutine.

opts.t: Empty string — ch receives before select
opts.why: The goroutine hasn't sent yet when select runs; it sends after 5 s.

why: select blocks until one of its cases is ready. time.After(1s) fires first because the goroutine sleeps for 5 s. The timeout arm runs and prints "timeout".
fj: "The goroutine sends first, so it prints done."
fs: "select races both cases; time.After wins because 1 s < 5 s — classic timeout pattern."

q: The unbuffered send deadlocks with no receiver. Pick the fix.

opts.t: Make the channel buffered: make(chan int, 1)
Move ch <- 42 into a goroutine: go func() { ch <- 42 }()
opts.why: Moving the send to a goroutine unblocks notify, but the goroutine leaks because nobody ever receives from ch.

opts.t: Replace ch <- 42 with close(ch)
opts.why: close signals closure, not a value send; it does not fix the original intent of sending 42.

why: An unbuffered channel blocks the sender until a receiver is ready. With no receiver, the goroutine deadlocks. A buffer of 1 lets the send complete immediately; add a receiver elsewhere when the value must be consumed.
fj: "Move the send to a goroutine — that unblocks the main function."
fs: "Buffer size 1 decouples sender from receiver; a goroutine-only fix just moves the leak."

q: What does this program print?

opts.t: 11 then 1
11 then 11
opts.why: := inside the if creates a new x scoped to the if block; outer x is unchanged.

opts.t: 1 then 1
opts.why: The if condition uses := which shadows x; the condition is true (11 > 5), so 11 is printed first.

opts.t: Compile error — x declared twice
opts.why: Go allows := to shadow an outer variable in an inner scope; it is legal and common.

why: The short variable declaration in the if initializer creates a new x scoped to that if block. Prints 11 inside the block, then the outer x (still 1) after. This := shadowing silently hides mutations.
fj: ":= inside if updates the outer variable."
fs: ":= in an if initializer always creates a new block-scoped variable; outer scope is untouched."

q: Does modifying v inside range change the slice?

opts.t: [1 2 3] — range gives a copy; nums is unchanged
[2 4 6] — v is a reference into nums
opts.why: range copies each element into v; mutating v does not affect the slice.

opts.t: Panic — cannot modify a range variable
opts.why: Modifying v is legal; it just has no effect on the original slice because v is a copy.

why: The range loop copies each element into v. Mutating v only changes the local copy. To modify elements in place use a numeric index: nums[i] *= 2.
fj: "v is an alias into the slice, so *= updates it."
fs: "range always copies the value; use the index to write back to the slice."

q: What is wrong with this WaitGroup usage?

opts.t: wg.Add(1) is inside the goroutine; wg.Wait() may return before all goroutines start
wg.Done() must be called before work(i) returns
opts.why: defer wg.Done() runs after work returns, which is correct; the issue is Add placement.

opts.t: sync.WaitGroup cannot be used inside goroutines
opts.why: WaitGroup is designed for goroutine coordination; the bug is the race on Add.

why: wg.Add must be called before the goroutine launches so the counter is incremented before Wait is reached. With Add inside the goroutine, the scheduler may run wg.Wait() before any goroutine calls Add, causing Wait to return immediately.
fj: "Add can go anywhere inside the goroutine."
fs: "Call wg.Add(1) before go func() so the counter is set before Wait can see zero."

q: This defer runs in a loop. What is the problem?
lines: if err := doWork(tx, id); err != nil {

opts.t: All Rollback calls are deferred until processAll returns, so DB connections accumulate across iterations
tx.Commit() should be deferred too
opts.why: Deferring Commit would run after Rollback, likely on an already-closed transaction.

opts.t: Rollback after Commit is harmless and this is idiomatic
opts.why: Post-commit Rollback is a no-op, but the real bug is accumulating open transactions.

why: defer is function-scoped, so tx.Rollback() for every iteration queues up and fires only when processAll returns. Each loop iteration holds an open transaction. Fix: wrap the body in a helper function or closure that returns an error, keeping defer scoped to that inner call.
fj: "defer at the top of a loop body runs at the end of each iteration."
fs: "defer is function-scoped; extract loop body to a function so defer fires per iteration."

q: This middleware leaks goroutines in production. What is the bug?

opts.t: When ctx is cancelled, the goroutine blocks on ch <- compute(ctx) forever because nobody reads ch
compute should not receive ctx; it causes a data race
opts.why: Passing ctx to compute is correct and idiomatic; the leak is the unbuffered channel after cancel.

opts.t: select should use default to avoid blocking
opts.why: A default case would return immediately before compute finishes; it does not fix the goroutine leak.

why: When the client disconnects, ctx.Done() fires and the handler returns. But compute(ctx) may still be running and tries to send on ch — nobody reads it anymore, so the goroutine leaks. Fix: make ch buffered (size 1) so the goroutine can send even after the handler has moved on.
fj: "The goroutine will stop because ctx is cancelled."
fs: "ctx cancellation stops compute eventually, but the send on ch still blocks; buffer ch with size 1."

q: A latency spike appears after this code runs for hours. What is the subtle bug?

opts.t: time.After creates a new timer each iteration; unevaluated timers accumulate until GC runs
doWork() blocks the select and delays the next tick
opts.why: doWork blocking delays timing but does not cause a memory leak or GC pressure over time.

opts.t: select has two cases so only one fires per iteration; the other leaks
opts.why: select always drains exactly one case; the issue is the Timer value created by time.After not being stopped.

why: time.After(d) allocates a time.Timer internally. If the ctx.Done() case fires first, the unused timer is not garbage-collected until it fires (500 ms later), but creating one per tight loop iteration causes rapid timer heap growth and GC pressure. Fix: create a time.NewTicker or use time.NewTimer outside the loop and Reset it.
fj: "time.After is just a channel; it's cleaned up immediately."
fs: "time.After allocates a Timer that only GCs after it fires; use a Ticker or NewTimer+Reset in loops."

q: A function processes a 1 GB log chunk and returns a 10-byte summary. Memory stays high. Why?

opts.t: The returned slice shares the backing array of log; the 1 GB array is pinned in memory
log[:10] allocates a new 10-byte slice; the original is freed normally
opts.why: Slicing never copies; the sub-slice header still points to the original backing array.

opts.t: The GC collects log once summarize returns because it is no longer referenced
opts.why: The returned sub-slice holds a reference to the backing array; GC cannot collect it.

why: log[:10] returns a slice header whose Data pointer points into the original 1 GB array. As long as the caller holds the result, the full array stays alive. Fix: return a copy — out := make([]byte, 10); copy(out, log[:10]); return out.
fj: "The sub-slice is 10 bytes, so only 10 bytes are kept."
fs: "Any sub-slice pins the entire backing array; always copy when the parent array is large."

q: A mutex is embedded in a struct passed by value. What goes wrong?

opts.t: update receives a copy of Cache; locking the copy does not protect the original
Embedding sync.Mutex is invalid; use a pointer field instead
opts.why: Embedding sync.Mutex is idiomatic Go; the bug is passing the struct by value, not the embedding style.

opts.t: c.Lock() will deadlock because Mutex is embedded
opts.why: Embedded Mutex promotion works correctly; no deadlock occurs — but the lock is on the copy, not the original.

why: sync.Mutex must not be copied after first use. Passing Cache by value copies the mutex state. The lock in update is on a separate copy, so concurrent callers of the original are unprotected. Fix: change the signature to func update(c *Cache).
fj: "Embedding a Mutex means locking works on any copy of the struct."
fs: "A copied Mutex is a different Mutex; always pass structs with embedded Mutexes by pointer."

q: This shared library function has an org-wide blast-radius flaw. What is it?

opts.t: handlers is a package-level mutable map with no synchronisation; concurrent Register/Dispatch calls race
Returning Handler directly is unsafe; callers should receive a copy
opts.why: Handler is an interface (a pointer-like value); returning it is fine. The race is on the unsynchronised map.

opts.t: The map should be initialised with make in an init() function
opts.why: The composite literal initialises the map correctly; the bug is the missing mutex, not the initialisation style.

why: A package-level map mutated by Register while Dispatch reads it is a data race under the Go memory model. Any service importing this library that calls Register (e.g., at startup) and Dispatch (e.g., at request time) concurrently will crash. Fix: protect with sync.RWMutex, or use sync.Map.
fj: "Package-level maps are safe because Go initialises them at startup."
fs: "Package-level mutable maps shared across goroutines require a RWMutex; a race here takes down every importing service."

q: A shared library uses init() to register itself. What is the org-wide risk?

opts.t: init() panics if the metrics host is unreachable, crashing every binary that imports this package
init() runs after main(), so the connection is established too late
opts.why: init() runs before main(); timing is not the problem here.

opts.t: net.Dial should not be called in init() because the network is not initialised yet
opts.why: The network stack is available at init() time; the risk is the unconditional panic on failure.

why: Putting I/O and panic inside init() is a library anti-pattern. Any binary — including test runners, CLIs, and microservices — that imports this package will panic at startup if metrics:9090 is unreachable. This is an org-wide availability blast radius. Libraries should expose an explicit Init(cfg) or New() that callers invoke deliberately and can handle errors from.
fj: "init() is the right place to set up shared connections so they're ready before main."
fs: "init() that panics on I/O failure crashes every importer; expose an explicit constructor that returns an error."

q: A shared HTTP helper spawns a goroutine per request. What is the systemic risk?

opts.t: Under high load each request spawns an unbounded goroutine; memory and scheduler pressure cause service-wide OOM
traceClient.Send may race with other goroutines reading r
opts.why: buildTrace copies needed fields before the goroutine diverges; the main issue is unbounded goroutine growth.

opts.t: The goroutine leaks because r is captured by reference and may outlive the request
opts.why: r is a pointer that outlives the handler; it does not leak. The scalability flaw is the unbounded goroutines.

why: Every request spawns a new goroutine with no backpressure. At 10k RPS the service creates 10k goroutines/s. Each goroutine costs ~2 KB stack; traceClient.Send adds latency variability. The fix is a bounded worker pool or a buffered channel queue with a fixed number of senders. Any service importing this helper inherits the behaviour.
fj: "Goroutines are cheap; spawning one per request is the idiomatic Go pattern."
fs: "Goroutines are cheap individually but unbounded spawn per request causes OOM at scale; use a bounded pool."

q: A shared error helper silently drops errors. What is the blast-radius risk?

opts.t: Errors are only logged; callers cannot detect failure, so silent data loss propagates org-wide
log.Printf is not safe for concurrent use and will race
opts.why: The standard log package is goroutine-safe; the problem is swallowing the error, not logger safety.

opts.t: MustExec should panic on error like database/sql conventions suggest
opts.why: Panicking on every DB error would crash request handlers; returning the error is the correct contract.

why: MustExec logs the error but returns nothing; every caller treats the mutation as successful. In audit trails, financial ledgers, or idempotency tables this means silent data loss. Naming it Must signals it will panic (Go convention), but it neither panics nor returns errors. Fix: rename to TryExec and return error, or panic explicitly if that is the intended contract. Org-wide adoption amplifies each silent failure.
fj: "Logging the error is enough; callers don't need to check it every time."
fs: "Swallowed errors are invisible to callers; in a shared library this silently corrupts data across every service that imports it."
